topic.title: Standard 11: Safeguarding Children
topic.summary: Understanding how to recognise signs of abuse or neglect in children and young people, knowing your responsibilities, and knowing how to report concerns.

questions.source_criterion: 11.1a List signs and symptoms of abuse and neglect in children and young people
questions.prompt: Which of the following could be a sign of physical abuse in a child?

options.label: Unexplained bruises, burns, or fractures that do not match the explanation given
The child always has clean clothes
The child is quiet and well-behaved
The child eats well at mealtimes

questions.explanation: Unexplained injuries, especially when the explanation does not match the injury, can be a sign of physical abuse. Other signs include frequent injuries, injuries in patterns (like handprint marks), or injuries in various stages of healing.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a potential sign of neglect in a child?

options.label: Being consistently dirty, hungry, or inappropriately dressed for the weather
Arriving at school on time every day
Having friends at school
Talking happily about their weekend

questions.explanation: Neglect is the persistent failure to meet a child's basic needs. Signs include poor hygiene, being consistently hungry, wearing clothes that are too small or unsuitable for the weather, and missing medical appointments.

questions.source_criterion: 11.1a List signs and symptoms of abuse
questions.prompt: What might indicate that a child is experiencing emotional abuse?

options.label: The child seems overly anxious, withdrawn, or shows extreme behaviour like being very aggressive or very compliant
The child sometimes argues with their siblings
The child is occasionally moody
The child has a small group of close friends

questions.explanation: Emotional abuse can cause children to become anxious, withdrawn, depressed, or to display extreme behaviours. They may seem overly affectionate to strangers or, conversely, have difficulty forming relationships. It can be harder to spot than physical signs.

questions.source_criterion: 11.2 Understand your responsibilities in relation to safeguarding children
questions.prompt: If you suspect a child is being abused or neglected, what should you do first?

options.label: Report your concerns to your manager or the designated safeguarding lead immediately
Investigate the situation yourself before reporting anything
Wait until you have more evidence before saying anything
Speak directly to the child's parents about your concerns

questions.explanation: Your responsibility is to report concerns, not to investigate them. You should report immediately to your manager or the designated safeguarding lead. Investigating yourself could make the situation worse and is not part of your role.

questions.source_criterion: 11.2 Understand your responsibilities in relation to safeguarding children
questions.prompt: What does 'working together to safeguard children' mean in practice?

options.label: All organisations and individuals involved with children sharing information and working collaboratively to protect them from harm
Each organisation working separately on child protection
Only social services being responsible for child protection
Keeping information about concerns completely confidential within your organisation

questions.explanation: 'Working Together to Safeguard Children' is the statutory guidance that sets out how organisations and individuals must work together to protect children. It emphasises the importance of information sharing and multi-agency collaboration.

questions.source_criterion: 11.3 Understand the rights of children and young people
questions.prompt: What document sets out the rights of all children and young people?

options.label: The United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC)
The Children Act 1989
The Equality Act 2010
The Human Rights Act 1998

questions.explanation: The UNCRC is an international treaty that sets out the civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights of every child, regardless of their background. It includes the right to be safe, to be heard, to have an education, and to have their views taken seriously.

questions.source_criterion: 11.3 Understand the rights of children and young people
questions.prompt: What does 'the right to be heard' mean for a child in a safeguarding situation?

options.label: The child has the right to express their views and have them taken seriously in decisions that affect them
The child can say whatever they want without any consequences
Only adults can speak in safeguarding meetings
The child's views are not important because adults know best

questions.explanation: The right to be heard (Article 12 of the UNCRC) means children have the right to express their views freely and have those views considered in all matters affecting them. This includes safeguarding situations.

questions.prompt: Where would you find your organisation's procedures for safeguarding children?

options.label: In your organisation's safeguarding policy, which should be accessible to all staff
Safeguarding procedures are confidential and only shared with managers
You do not need to read them — your manager will tell you if something changes
From the local authority's website only

questions.explanation: Your organisation should have a safeguarding policy that is available to all staff. You should be familiar with it as part of your induction and training. It will outline your responsibilities and the procedures to follow if you have concerns.

questions.source_criterion: 11.5 Understand how to respond to a disclosure
questions.prompt: A child tells you they are being hurt at home. What should you do?

options.label: Listen calmly, do not promise to keep it secret, tell them you need to tell someone who can help, and report it immediately
Promise not to tell anyone so they trust you
Ask lots of detailed questions to get the full story
Tell them it is probably not that bad

questions.explanation: If a child discloses abuse, stay calm, listen without interrupting, do not promise secrecy (explain you must tell someone who can help), do not ask leading questions, and report immediately to your safeguarding lead. Your response can significantly affect the child's wellbeing.

questions.prompt: Do safeguarding responsibilities for children apply to all health and social care workers, even if they primarily work with adults?

options.label: Yes — all workers have a responsibility to safeguard children, regardless of their main role
No — only workers in children's services need to know about safeguarding children
Only if children visit the care setting regularly
Only managers have safeguarding responsibilities for children

questions.explanation: All health and social care workers have a responsibility to safeguard children and young people, regardless of their main role. You may encounter children as visitors, or become aware of concerns through an adult you support who has children.

questions.source_criterion: 11.6 Summarise key legislation relating to safeguarding children
questions.prompt: Which key piece of UK legislation sets out the legal framework for safeguarding children?

options.label: The Children Act 1989 (and 2004 amendments)
The Mental Capacity Act 2005
The Health and Social Care Act 2012
The Data Protection Act 2018

questions.explanation: The Children Act 1989 (amended by the Children Act 2004) is the key legislation for safeguarding children in England. It sets out the duties of local authorities, courts, and other agencies to protect children's welfare.

questions.prompt: A child frequently arrives at school tired, hungry, and in the same dirty clothes. What type of abuse or neglect is this most likely to indicate?

options.label: Neglect — the persistent failure to meet the child's basic physical and psychological needs
Physical abuse
Sexual abuse
Emotional abuse

questions.explanation: This pattern strongly suggests neglect, which is the most common form of child maltreatment. Neglect involves the ongoing failure to meet a child's basic needs for food, clothing, shelter, supervision, and medical care.
